paper_title: Personality Shapes Gender Bias in Persona-Conditioned LLM Narratives Across English and Hindi: An Empirical Investigation

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in persona-driven applications such as education, customer service, and social platforms, where models are prompted to adopt specific personas when interacting with users. While persona conditioning can improve user experience and engagement, it also raises concerns about how personality cues may interact with gender biases and stereotypes. In this work, we present a controlled study of persona-conditioned story generation in English and Hindi, where each story portrays a working professional in India producing context-specific artifacts (e.g., lesson plans, reports, letters) under systematically varied persona gender, occupational role, and personality traits from the HEXACO and Dark Triad frameworks. Across 23,400 generated stories from six state-of-the-art LLMs, we find that personality traits are significantly associated with both the magnitude and direction of gender bias. In particular, Dark Triad personality traits are consistently associated with higher gender-stereotypical representations compared to socially desirable HEXACO traits, though these associations vary across models and languages. Our findings demonstrate that gender bias in LLMs is not static but context-dependent. This suggests that persona-conditioned systems used in real-world applications may introduce uneven representational harms, reinforcing gender stereotypes in generated educational, professional, or social content.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Large Language Models (LLMs) have become ubiquitous in persona-driven and role-playing applications, where users prompt models to adopt specific identities such as professions, demographics, or psychological profiles, for more engaging Equal contribution Work done outside role at the authors respective companies. 1 Figure 1: Illustration of personality modulation of gender-stereotypical language in LLM-generated occupational artifacts. Male: Baseline shows eﬀicient chores; personality conditioning shifts to strategic delegation for personal gain. Female: Baseline emphasizes dutiful cleaning/cooking; personality conditioning redirects to impression management and favor-seeking. responses (Lutz et al., 2025). While persona conditioning improves perceived realism, prior work shows that it also influences stereotyping, for example, interview-style prompting and name-based priming can reduce stereotypical outputs, highlighting how persona cues can shape, amplify, or suppress latent social biases in model behaviour (Gupta et al., 2024). When prompted to adopt personas, LLMs do not merely change style; they change how social categories are expressed. Gender biases persist in LLM-generated narratives, where characters roles, agency, and portrayals often vary systematically with gender (Chen et al., 2025a; Masoudian et al., 2025). However, while existing work has studied bias under variations of prompts, roles, or demographic attributes, how personality traits may modulate the expression of stereotypes is still underexplored. In human psychology, stereotype expression is highly context-dependent and shaped by personality traits and social norms. Antagonistic traits (Dark Triad) increase stereotyping, while prosocial traits suppress it (Greitemeyer and Kastenmüller, 2023). If LLMs internalize statistical regularities from human language and behaviour, conditioning models on psychologically grounded personality traits may surface systematic personality-gender interaction effects. Language further complicates bias analysis. Gender bias varies substantially across languages, shaped by grammatical gender systems and cultural context (Rowe et al., 2025). Hindi, the worlds third most spoken language (eth, 2025), encodes gender through adjective agreement and verb morphology rather than pronouns (Hall, 2016), and prior work on Hindi shows gender bias interacts with occupation, social hierarchy, and morphology in both representations and generation (Malik et al., 2022; Gupta et al., 2025; Hada et al., 2024). Yet, personality-conditioned narrative generation in Hindi remains largely unexplored. In this work, we investigate how personality conditioning influences gender bias in personadriven LLM interactions. We conduct large-scale controlled study of persona-conditioned story generation in English and Hindi. We systematically vary persona gender, occupational role (see Appendix A.2), and personality traits from the HEXACO and Dark Triad frameworks, generating 23, 400 artifacts across six diverse Language Models (LMs). To quantify gender bias in these artifacts, we introduce sentence-level embedding-based metric that measures semantic alignment with male and female-stereotypical reference centroids, enabling bias localization within artifacts (Caliskan et al., 2017; May et al., 2019). We address the following research questions: RQ1: Are personality traits associated with different levels of gender bias in LLMgenerated artifacts? RQ2: Do personality and gender interact to amplify or attenuate gender bias? RQ3: How do these interaction effects differ across languages? Our analysis reveals that gender bias in LLMgenerated narratives is not static, but strongly context-dependent. Dark Triad traits consistently amplify representations, while prosocial traits tend to attenuate them. These gender-stereotypical effects persist across models, occupations, and languages, and in many cases exceed the influence of the explicit gender label itself. We also observe distinct patterns in Hindi compared to English, reflecting interactions between personality conditioning and grammatical gender marking. Our contributions are: large multilingual corpus of personalityconditioned artifacts spanning gender, occupation, and psychological traits. sentence-level centroid-based metric for quantifying gender bias in artifact generation. Empirical evidence that personality traits act as systematic bias amplifiers or attenuators in LLM outputs. Analysis across languages, occupations, and model architectures, highlighting implications for bias evaluation in persona-driven applications. The code and datasets to replicate our analyses will be made available on GitHub1 and Hugging Face

sections.title: Generation
sections.content: Prior work has extensively shown that language models encode gender stereotypes, associating men and women with different occupations, traits, and social roles. Early embedding-based analyses and benchmark datasets (e.g., WEAT/SEAT, WinoBias, StereoSet, CrowS-Pairs) demonstrate that such biases persist across representations and downstream tasks (Caliskan et al., 2017; May et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2018; Nadeem et al., 2021; Nangia et al., 2020). As LMs became more generative, studies shifted toward narrative and discourse-level bias revealing that gender bias often manifests through differences in agency, sentiment, and evaluative framing. Male characters are portrayed as more agentic and authoritative, while female characters are described using more communal or emotional language (Lucy and Bamman, 2021; Wan et al., 2023; Masoudian et al., 2025; Kong et al., 2024) with masculine defaults persisting even under gender-neutral prompts (Doyen and Todirascu, 2025; Teleki et al., 2025). 1Anonymous Code Repository 2Anonymous Data Repository 2 Gender bias also varies across languages. Hindi and other Indic languages remain underexplored despite their large speaker populations (Rowe et al., 2025; eth, 2025). Existing work shows that Hindi gender bias interacts with grammatical gender, occupation, and social hierarchy, and that Englishcentric bias measures do not transfer directly to gender-marking languages (Malik et al., 2022; Gupta et al., 2025; Hada et al., 2024; Hall, 2016). However, these studies do not consider personaor personality-conditioned generation.

sections.title: 2.2 Persona Conditioning, Personality Traits
sections.content: and Bias Modulation Persona prompting significantly shapes LLM behaviour, affecting tone, style, and content, and prompt formulation itself can influence stereotyping (Lutz et al., 2025). LLMs have also been shown to exhibit stable patterns along psychological dimensions such as the Big Five, HEXACO, and Dark Triad (Salecha et al., 2024; Zheng et al., 2025). Critically, personality conditioning can modulate harmful behaviours, including toxicity and bias, with certain personality traits acting as amplifiers and others as attenuators (Wang et al., 2025; Li et al., 2024). Persona-based prompts have also been shown to elicit implicit reasoning biases even when surface outputs appear neutral (Gupta et al., 2024). However, this literature typically examines personality or gender separately, without modeling their interactions across occupational context or language.

sections.title: 2.3 Position of This Work
sections.content: Prior work establishes that LLMs exhibit gender bias in narratives and that persona and personality prompts shape model behaviour. What remains unexplored is how personality traits modulate gender bias through interaction effects, particularly in multilingual narrative generation grounded in occupational contexts. Our work addresses this gap by systematically varying gender, occupation, and HEXACO and Dark Triad personality traits in English and Hindi story generation. Unlike prior studies, we examine personalitygender interactions and measure story-level bias using sentence-based approach.

sections.title: 3 Methods
sections.content: Our experimental pipeline is illustrated in Figure 2. (1) The pipeline consists of three major stages: Corpus Creation, in which multilingual artifacts are generated using multiple LLMs from personaconditioned prompts specifying gender, occupation and personality traits; (2) Stereotype Centroid Construction, where gender stereotype centroids are derived from curated word lists; (3) Bias Scoring in which story-level bias scores are computed based on their similarity to stereotype centroids.

sections.content: Occupation. To situate personas in realistic socio-cultural settings, we compile list of 50 occupations relevant to the Indian context (Ministry of Labour & Employment, Government of India, 2015). Following prior work on occupational gender stereotypes (Bolukbasi et al., 2016; Zhao et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2025a,b), we select 25 predominantly male-stereotyped occupations (e.g., carpenter, auto-rickshaw driver) and 25 predominantly female-stereotyped occupations (e.g., nurse, beautician). Each occupation (e.g., Teacher) is paired with (i) an artifact (e.g., lesson plan) and (ii) brief scenario that specifies the situational context for generation (e.g., design lesson integrating group work to enhance student collaboration ). See Appendix A.2 for more details. Language. Since our occupational scenarios are grounded in the Indian socio-cultural context, we include Hindi as primary language of analysis. Hindi is the most widely spoken language in India and third most spoken globally. To enable comparison with prior LLM studies and widely used evaluation setting, we also include English as second language. Personality Traits. We operationalize personality using the HEXACO model (Honesty-Humility, Emotionality, Extraversion, Agreeableness, Conscientiousness, Openness) and the Dark Triad framework (Machiavellianism, Narcissism, Psychopathy), yielding 9 distinct personality traits. For each trait, we construct high-score and lowscore textual descriptions resulting in 18 distinct 3 Figure 2: step-by-step illustration of our experimental pipeline. Persona-conditioned prompts specifying gender, occupation, and personality traits are used to generate artifacts from multiple LLMs. The generated artifacts are embedded in multilingual sentence space, and gender stereotype centroids are constructed from curated stereotype word lists. Story-level bias scores are computed based on cosine similarity to these centroids and used in the subsequent analysis. personality conditions. Full textual descriptions are provided in Appendix A.1. We adopt HEXACO descriptions from prior work (Wang et al., 2025) and parallel descriptions were designed for Dark Triad traits. To validate these prompts, we administer the Short Dark Triad (SD3) Test (Jones and Paulhus, 2014) on outputs from the GPT 5.2 model and confirm alignment with the targeted personality profiles. Full details are provided in Appendix A.1.1.

Produce the {Artifact} in {Language} as moderate-length paragraph (68 meaningful sentences) that fully reflects this personas thoughts, emotions, and worldview shaped by their personality and situation. This template ensures that generated artifacts consistently reflect the specified persona attributes while maintaining realistic narrative context.

sections.title: Measurement
sections.content: We represent gender stereotypes in shared embedding space using male and female centroids derived from gender-stereotypical language. Intuitively, the male centroid encodes traits such as being leader-like, ambitious, and assertive, while the female centroid encodes traits like nurturing, empathetic, cooperative, and graceful (Fast et al., 2016; Bolukbasi et al., 2016). This enables us to quantify how closely given sentence aligns with maleor female-coded stereotypes in both English and Hindi. We construct the respective centroids in three steps. Step 1: Stereotypical word lists: We manually compile gender-stereotypical word lists in English following prior work on occupational and traitbased gender bias (Fast et al., 2016; Hoyle et al., 2019). Each list contains approximately 200 210 items, covering nouns, verbs, and adjectives (e.g., engineer, decisive, dominates for male; caregiver, empathetic, supports for female). We then translate these words into Hindi and iteratively refine with native speakers to ensure stereotypical validity in Hindi usage. We also check these words against prior works (Malik et al., 2022; Kirtane and Anand, 2022). This design choice keeps the English and Hindi lexicons parallel, so cross-lingual comparisons are not confounded by differing stereotype sets. Step 2: Sentence Templates: To obtain contextualized representations, we convert each stereotype word into one or more short sentences using part-ofspeech-specific templates (e.g., This is _, That is _ etc. in English, यह _ ह, वह _ ह etc. in Hindi). Applying these templates yields roughly 1,000 male-stereotypical sentences and 1,100 femalestereotypical sentences across both English and Hindi. Step 3: Centroid Computation: Since our analysis spans both English and Hindi in an Indian context, we use the multilingual sentence embedding model indic-sentence-similarity-sbert4 released by L3Cube for all centroid and artifact representations. This ensures that English and Hindi sentences inhabit shared semantic space that is tailored to Indic languages. Let {smale }Nm i=1 and {sfemale }Nf j=1 denote the sentence embeddings for maleand femalestereotypical sentences, respectively. We compute the male and female centroids as the mean of their corresponding embeddings: cmale =

sections.title: 3.3 Bias Metric and Story-Level Aggregation
sections.content: For each artifact S, we first segment the text into sentences = {s1, s2, . . . , sn}. We then embed each sentence using the same multilingual sentence encoder described in Section 3.2, yielding vectors si for = 1, . . . , n. Given the male and female centroids cmale and cfemale, we define sentence-level bias score as: bias(si) = cos(si, cmale) cos(si, cfemale) where cos(, ) denotes cosine similarity. By construction, bias(si) [1, 1]. Positive values indicate male-stereotypical language alignment and 3We release the full corpus together with code for reproduc4Model details are available at https://huggingface.co/ ing all experiments. l3cube-pune/indic-sentence-similarity-sbert 5 negative values indicate female-stereotypical language alignment. This difference-of-cosines metric is standard in embedding-based bias measurement (Caliskan et al., 2017; May et al., 2019). To obtain single bias score per artifact, we aggregate sentence-level scores by selecting the sentence with the maximum absolute bias: story(S) = bias(sk), = arg max (cid:12) (cid:12)bias(si) (cid:12) (cid:12). This prioritizes the most strongly biased statement in the artifact, which is likely most psychologically salient to readers impressions of the persona (see Appendix A.6 for robustness checks). All subsequent analyses in Section 3.5 use story(S) as the primary outcome variable.

sections.title: Annotation
sections.content: three To validate our sentence-level bias score, annotators independently evaluated 100 story pairs (50 per language, 200 stories in total), selecting which story in each pair exhibited greater gender stereotyping. Each pair contrasted personalityconditioned narrative against neutral baseline, with both stories sharing the same occupation and gender assignment. Inter-annotator agreement was substantial in both languages (Fleiss κ = 0.66 for English, κ = 0.69 for Hindi; Landis and Koch 1977), indicating reliable and The consistent judgements across annotators. personality-conditioned story was identified as more stereotyped in 66% of English pairs and 72% of Hindi pairs under majority vote, with unanimous agreement in 76% and 82% of cases respectively. Taken together, these results suggest that the sentence-level bias score provides robust signal of gender-stereotypical content across both languages.

sections.title: 4.1 Baseline
sections.content: Across all LLMs, stories generated without personality conditioning exhibit consistent malestereotypical bias in both English and Hindi (full results available in the Appendix Table 15), with Hindi generally showing stronger male-leaning skew across five of six models, suggesting that grammatical gender marking and cultural context amplify stereotypical alignment. This replicates prior findings that LLMs default to male-coded language in occupational contexts (Kotek et al., 2023; Chen et al., 2025a). Notably, across all LLMs, stories with personality conditioning exhibit consistently higher male-stereotypical bias. 6 Figure 5: Gender coeﬀicient effects on story-level bias scores relative to the neutral baseline. Male personas shift bias positively; female personas shift it negatively, with the male effect larger in absolute magnitude. Figure 6: Gender stratified personality effects on bias scores. Male personas (blue): Dark Triad show stronger positive shifts. The amplification is more pronounced for male-gendered personas. alignment. When both gender and personality are included in the regression model, several personality coefficients (Appendix Figure 17) are larger in magnitude than those associated with gender. In particular, Machiavellianism, Psychopathy, Agreeableness, and Honesty-Humility produce larger shifts, suggesting that personality conditioning can have stronger effect on gender-stereotypical alignment than the gender label alone. Gender-stratified analyses, in which we fit separate regression models for each persona gender (Figure 6), further show that the direction of personality effects is consistent across male and female personas, but their magnitude is systematically larger for male personas. This pattern is especially pronounced for Machiavellianism and Psychopathy, suggesting that Dark Triad traits compound male gender cues in amplifying stereotypical alignment. Together, these findings provide partial support for RQ2: although both gender and personality influence bias, personality can meaningfully amplify or attenuate the expression of gendered language. Figure 4: Personality trait effects on story-level gender bias scores, where positive score means malestereotypical alignment. Dark Triad traits produce positive shifts; few HEXACO traits show negative shifts.

sections.title: 4.2 Personality as a Bias Modulator (RQ1)
sections.content: Personality traits are associated with different direction and magnitude of gender bias in the generated output. As shown in Figure 4, assigning Dark Triad traits (Machiavellianism, Psychopathy) consistently produces positive shifts in bias scores, indicating stronger alignment with male-stereotypical language, while Narcissism shows more modest effect. Among the HEXACO traits, the pattern is heterogeneous. Agreeableness, HonestyHumility, and Conscientiousness also produce positive shifts of comparable magnitude, whereas Openness to Experience and Emotionality exhibit negative shifts, indicating attenuation toward female-stereotypical alignment, and Extraversion shows no significant effect. Personality conditioning consistently increases the proportion of male-leaning artifacts relative to the no-personality baseline across all models, as shown in Figure 3. The per-model distributions (see Figures 2132 in the Appendix), further illustrate the effect of adding personality in the prompt compared to the baseline. Overall, these findings address RQ1 by indicating that personality traits are indeed associated with different levels of gender bias, and do not act as merely stylistic modifiers.

sections.title: 4.3 Gender Effects (RQ2)
sections.content: We now evaluate the relative and joint contributions of gender and personality. As shown in Figure 5, in regression that includes only gender as categorical predictor, the male persona receives positive coeﬀicient relative to the neutral baseline, whereas the female persona receives negative coeﬀicient, indicating that explicit gender labeling alone modulates stereotypical 7 consistent with the more female-leaning baselines (see Table 15 in the Appendix). At the same time, the direction of personality effects remains consistent across architectures: Dark Triad traits amplify male-stereotypical alignment, whereas prosocial traits attenuate it. This suggests that the overall pattern of personality-driven modulation is not specific to single model family, but may reflect broader property of LLMs under persona conditioning.

sections.title: 5 Discussion and Conclusion
sections.content: We presented controlled study of personaconditioned story generation in English and Hindi, varying persona gender, occupation, and HEXACO and Dark Triad traits across 23,400 artifacts from six LLMs. Using sentence-level centroid-based bias metric validated by human annotation, we show that gender bias is contextdependent rather than fixed: Dark Triad traits consistently amplify male-stereotypical alignment, HEXACO traits have mixed effects, and in some cases personality effects exceed those of explicit gender labels. We also find that Hindi has stronger baseline male-stereotypical skew, while personality-driven modulation is more pronounced in English. Together, these results highlight personality prompts as fairness-critical design parameters in persona-driven multilingual systems. These findings carry broader implications for If personalpersona-driven LLM deployment. ity cues can systematically amplify or attenuate gender-stereotypical language, then bias should not be understood as static model artifact, but as an emergent property of the interaction between model, prompt design, and linguistic context. In practical applications such as educational support, workplace writing assistance, and customer service, persona specifications may therefore act as fairness-critical controls that shape how professions, competencies, and social roles are represented to users. The cross-linguistic differences we observe further suggest that mitigation strategies cannot be assumed to transfer uniformly across languages. Future work could extend persona-conditioned analysis beyond gender to other social categories (e.g., caste, religion, disability), as well as to multiturn interactions in which personas evolve over time, to support richer evaluations and provide clearer guidance for safer persona design. Figure 7: Language stratified personality effects. English (blue): Stronger positive bias shifts, esp. Dark Triad. English shows greater personality-driven malestereotypical amplification.

sections.title: 4.4 Cross-Linguistic Differences (RQ3)
sections.content: We now examine differences across English and Hindi. Hindi generations exhibit stronger baseline skew toward male-stereotypical alignment across models, suggesting that grammatical gender marking and sociocultural context may increase the default level of stereotypical bias (Appendix Table 15). However, personality-driven modulation appears more pronounced in English than in Hindi. As shown in Figure 7, Dark Triad traits yield stronger amplification effects in English, while attenuation under Openness and Emotionality is also more evident. Hindi follows the same overall directional pattern across traits, but with consistently smaller magnitudes. This pattern is further supported by the per-model analyses and the language-specific density plots provided in the Appendix (see Figures 1920 and Figures 2132), which show larger distributional shifts in English and more compressed, overlapping distributions in Hindi. These findings provide support for RQ3, showing that personality-driven modulation of gender bias is not uniform across languages but varies by linguistic context.

sections.title: 4.5 Model-Level Variation
sections.content: Lastly, we examine variation across model families. All models exhibit more female-stereotypical alignment relative to GPT-5 nano, which serves as the reference (see Figure 18 of the Appendix). The largest negative shifts are observed for Gemma3-1b-it and Falcon-Mamba-7b, whereas the other instruction-tuned models remain closer to the GPT5 nano baseline. This pattern suggests that smaller or architecturally distinct models may exhibit weaker male-stereotypical defaults in generation,

sections.title: Limitations
sections.content: Our study focuses on single task family, which is, occupation-grounded artifact generation in an Indian context, and fixed set of stereotyped occupations, which may limit generalization to other domains or interaction styles. The personality frameworks used (HEXACO and Dark Triad) originate from Western personality psychology and may not fully capture personality constructs across Indian cultural contexts. Bias is measured using centroid-based embedding metric with manually curated stereotype lexicons, making the results dependent on the chosen encoder and lexical sets. The stereotype word lists, though refined with native speakers, were originally compiled in English and translated into Hindi, which may introduce translation artifacts or fail to capture Hindi-specific stereotypical expressions that have no direct English equivalent. Personality prompt validation via the SD3 test was conducted on GPT-5 nano only; the degree to which the same prompts reliably elicit targeted personality profiles in architecturally distinct models (e.g., Falcon-Mamba SSM, DeepSeek-R1 LRM) was not independently verified. Methodologically, we treat each generated story as an independent sample and evaluate only six contemporary models, leaving open how persona-driven bias may manifest in longer conversations or in newer architectures. Our bias metric aggregates to single sentencelevel maximum score per story, which captures the most salient stereotypical statement but discards information about the overall distributional tendency of the artifact. Alternative aggregation strategies (e.g., mean score, proportion of biased sentences) may yield different conclusions. Furthermore, our metric measures alignment with pre-defined stereotypical centroids and does not capture more subtle or intersectional forms of bias, such as casteor class-based stereotyping that may co-occur with gender in Indian occupational contexts. The human annotation study involved only three annotators evaluating 100 story pairs, which, while showing substantial inter-annotator agreement, represents limited sample relative to the full corpus of 23, 400 artifacts. Annotation was also limited to pairwise comparisons against neutral baseline, and did not assess absolute stereotyping severity or capture annotator demographic variation that may influence stereotype perception. Finally, our controlled prompts simulate persona effects but do not reflect real-world chatbot usage patterns or user query distributions. Personality conditioning in deployment settings is rarely as explicit or structured as our prompts, and users may induce similar effects through more subtle cues. We also do not measure real-world consequences using live user data, nor do we investigate whether similar patterns arise in other languages or cultural contexts.

sections.title: Ethical Considerations
sections.content: This work studies gender bias in LLM-generated narratives and involves the construction of dataset containing gender-stereotypical language. While such content is necessary for measuring bias, we acknowledge that compiling and releasing stereotype lexicons and persona-conditioned narratives carries inherent risks, particularly of reinforcing the very stereotypes we seek to expose. To mitigate this, we contextualize all examples within an explicit bias measurement framework and release the dataset with documentation discouraging its use for stereotype generation outside of fairness research. Our study conditions language models on Dark Triad personality traits (Machiavellianism, Narcissism, Psychopathy) to examine bias amplification. We caution that prompts designed to elicit such behaviors could be misused to systematically generate harmful, manipulative, or stereotyped content at scale. Researchers and practitioners using our dataset or prompt templates should be aware of this risk and implement appropriate safeguards. The human annotation study involved three annotators who evaluated narratives containing genderstereotypical language. Annotators were briefed on the nature of the task prior to participation, informed that the content was machine-generated and does not reflect the views of the authors, and were free to withdraw at any time. Annotators were compensated fairly for their time. Our occupational scenarios are grounded in the Indian socio-cultural context and reflect existing societal gender stereotypes associated with specific professions in India. We do not intend to endorse these stereotypes; rather, they are drawn from documented occupational gender associations to create ecologically valid test conditions. The personality frameworks used (HEXACO and Dark Triad) originate from Western psychology and may not fully represent personality constructs across all cultural contexts, limitation we acknowledge explicitly. 9 Finally, all experiments were conducted using publicly available or API-accessible language models. No private user data was collected or used at any stage of this study. We used AI-based writing assistants to improve clarity and language in the manuscript; however, all research ideas, methodological decisions, analyses, and interpretations are solely those of the authors.

sections.title: A Appendix
sections.content: This section provides supplementary material in the form of additional examples, implementation details, etc. to bolster the readers understanding of the concepts presented in this work. A.1 Personality Traits Descriptions The HEXACO and Dark Triad personality frameworks were used to characterize individual differences in personality traits relevant to social behavior and interpersonal dynamics. The HEXACO model captures six broad dimensions of personality, namely, HonestyHumility, Emotionality, Extraversion, Agreeableness, Conscientiousness, and Openness to Experience, representing prosocial tendencies, emotional responses, and cognitive styles. In contrast, the Dark Triad framework focuses on three socially aversive traits, namely, Machiavellianism, Narcissism, and Psychopathy, associated with manipulative strategies, selfenhancement, and impulsive or callous behavior. Tables 5 and 6 present descriptive summaries illustrating typical behavioral tendencies associated with relatively high and low levels of each trait. A.1.1 SD3 (Short Dark Triad) Validation SD3 Test Item List The following SD3 items were used to evaluate whether prompted personality descriptions successfully induced the intended Dark Triad traits in model outputs. Items marked (R) are reversescored.

Ill say anything to get what want. Validation of SD3 Personality Descriptions We first constructed high-score and low-score prompts for each Dark Triad trait. To verify that these prompts induced the intended personality tendencies in the generated outputs, we evaluated model responses using the SD3 inventory above. Aggregate trait scores were computed and compared across high and low prompt conditions. A.2 Occupational Prompt Design Gender-Stereotype Classification of Occupations The occupations listed in Table 7 are categorized based on widely observed gender stereotypes in the Indian socio-cultural context, following prior work on occupational gender bias in language models and social perception (Bolukbasi et al., 2016; Zhao et al., 2018). Specifically, we construct two sets of 25 occupations each, representing predominantly male-stereotyped and female-stereotyped roles. These categorizations are not intended to reflect actual workforce distributions or normative judgments, but rather to capture commonly held societal associations that may influence language model behavior. In the Indian context, occupational roles are often strongly gender-coded due to historical labor divisions and socio-cultural norms (Malik et al., 2022; 12 High-Score Description Low-Score Description Machiavellianism Narcissism Psychopathy Total (Overall) 44/45 27/45 27/ 98/135 12/45 25/45 18/45 55/135 Table 2: SD3 validation scores for Machiavellianism Descriptions High-Score Description Low-Score Description Machiavellianism Narcissism Psychopathy Total (Overall) 25/45 44/45 19/45 88/135 16/45 10/45 13/45 39/135 Table 3: SD3 validation scores for Narcissism Descriptions stract descriptions, thereby improving ecological validity. the Importantly, artifactscenario pairings standardize the task across conditions, allowing us to control for contextual variation while systematically manipulating persona attributes such as gender and personality. As outlined in Section 3.1, these mappings are used consistently across both baseline and personality-conditioned prompts, enabling us to isolate the effects of personality traits on gender bias beyond occupational priors. By anchoring each generation in concrete task (e.g., reports, plans, logs), the setup reduces variability in output structure and facilitates more reliable comparison of bias across occupations, languages, and models. Gupta et al., 2024). For example, professions such as construction work, driving, and mechanical labor are commonly associated with men, whereas caregiving, domestic work, and service-oriented roles are more frequently associated with women. Prior work has shown that such associations are reflected and often amplified in language technologies, particularly in multilingual settings involving Hindi and English. Each occupation is paired with corresponding artifact and scenario (e.g., lesson plans, reports, logs), providing contextual grounding for generation and ensuring ecological validity of the produced narratives. As described in Section 3.1, these occupations are used both in baseline prompts (without personality conditioning) and in persona-conditioned settings, allowing us to isolate the contribution of personality traits to gender bias beyond occupational priors. We emphasize that these labels represent stereotypes rather than ground truth, and are included solely to facilitate bias measurement. The goal is not to reinforce such associations, but to systematically evaluate how they are reproduced or amplified in LLM-generated content. OccupationArtifactScenario Specifications Tables 8 and 9 present the full set of occupation artifactscenario mappings used in our personaconditioned generation pipeline. The occupations are divided into predominantly male-stereotyped and female-stereotyped categories (as described earlier), with each role paired with task-specific artifact and short contextual scenario. Each occupation is paired with task-specific artifact and contextual scenario grounded in the Indian socio-cultural setting. 18 A.3 Model Selection Rationale To ensure that our findings are not specific to single model architecture, we evaluate diverse set of contemporary language models spanning different design paradigms and capability levels. The selected models, that are, GPT-5 nano, Llama3.3-70B-Instruct, Gemma-3-1B-it, DeepSeek-R1, and Falcon-Mamba-7BMixtral-8x7B-Instruct, Instruct represent different architectural families, including standard transformer-based LLMs, small language models, reasoning-oriented models, mixture-of-experts architectures, and state-space models. This diversity allows us to examine whether personality-conditioned gender bias patterns generalize across fundamentally different model designs rather than being an artifact of specific architecture. We include models with different parameter sizes and training objectives to assess whether the interaction between personality conditioning and gender bias is consistent across models with different capacities and inductive biases.

sections.title: Choice of Sentence Embedding Model
sections.content: Our bias metric relies on sentence embeddings to measure semantic similarity between generated artifacts and gender-stereotypical reference centroids. Since our study focuses on Hindi and Indian occupational contexts, we use IndicSBERT, multilingual sentence encoder trained specifically on large collection of Indian languages. IndicSBERT has been shown to better capture semantic relationships in Indian languages compared to general-purpose multilingual encoders (Deode et al., 2023). We also experimented with LaBSE, widely used multilingual sentence embedding model (Feng et al., 2022). However, preliminary experiments indicated that LaBSE produced less distinct separation between maleand female-aligned similarity distributions across personality conditions. In contrast, IndicSBERT yielded clearer and more stable bias patterns, hence, we use IndicSBERT as the primary encoder in our bias computation pipeline. A.4 Personality Conditioned Generated

sections.title: Narratives
sections.content: Figure 8 presents narratives generated under identical High-Score Psychopathy conditioning for female and male domestic worker. Despite the same personality prompt, the model filters the trait through gender-specific behavioral scripts. In the female narrative, highlighted segments cluster around covert, calculated harm: the narrator plans to exploit her access to household resources by selling cooking oil for personal profit while lying to conceal it, deliberately sabotages the employers property for amusement (accidentally shrink few of their favorite clothes), and most severely, intends to tamper with food to cause physical discomfort (slip in some extra spices to give them little stomachache, just for kicks) which is the only instance of intended bodily harm across both stories. Psychopathy is thus enacted through the tools of domestic labor itself, weaponizing the feminized role from within. In the male narrative, the highlighted segments instead mark overt self-indulgence and authority defiance: unauthorized spending (order some chicken biryani... let the household pay for it), casual rule-breaking (break few rules), and direct dominance assertion (show them whos boss), rationalized through an economic frame (I dont get paid enough to deal with their nonsense). The male character does not harm the employer, he simply refuses to serve them, treating the domestic role as structurally beneath his entitlement. These divergences are not incidental. They map onto well-documented cultural stereotypes of gendered aggression: female antisocial behavior as covert, relational, and retributive; male antisocial behavior as overt, self-serving, and dismissive. The figure illustrates how personality conditioning does not operate in gender-neutral manner but restructures behavioral expression along stereotyped lines. This demonstrates why close qualitative analysis is essential: surface-level indicators of rule-breaking would obscure the substantive differences in severity, modality, and moral framing. Figure 9 presents Hindi narratives generated under identical High-Score Psychopathy conditioning for female and male babysitter. Despite the same personality prompt, the model routes the trait through two distinct gendered scripts, structured by the same cultural logic observed in the English corpus. In the female narrative, highlighted segments reveal pattern of covert neglect and deliberate concealment: the narrator knowingly feeds the child inappropriate food (I knew it wouldnt be good for them, but didnt care), reframes her indifference as the childs autonomy (it is important that the child be able to make decisions), and withholds information from the parents. A.5 Gendered Stereotypical Words Validation of Gender Associations in the Embedding Space To quantify gender associations in the embedding space, we computed WEAT-style Cohens effect size measuring the differential association between male and female target words and gender-associated attribute sentences. Target word embeddings were generated using the IndicSBERT model. For each target word, an association score was calculated as the difference between its mean cosine similarity with male attribute sentences and its mean cosine similarity with female attribute sentences. Attribute sentences were constructed by inserting gender-associated adjectives and verbs into simple sentence templates (e.g., This is _). The effect size was then computed as the difference between the mean association scores of the male and female target word groups, normalized by the pooled standard deviation of all association scores. All embeddings were L2-normalized prior to cosine similarity computation. The resulting Cohens effect sizes indicate strong gender association patterns in both Hindi (d = 0.9302) and English (d = 0.9447), both exceeding the conventional threshold for large effect size (d > 0.80). These results suggest that gender-associated semantic patterns are strongly encoded in the models multilingual embedding space for both languages. A.6 Robustness Check: Aggregation Strategy Max-abs targets the peak stereotyping signal per story, while mean and median summarize central tendency across all sentences including near-zero background sentences. Despite this difference, max-abs and mean agree on bias direction (malevs. female-leaning) in 87.2% of stories (Cohens κ = 0.738), and max-abs and top-3 mean in 87.7% (κ = 0.749), both indicating substantial agreement (Landis and Koch, 1977), with disagreements uniformly distributed across traits and models. These results confirm that the choice of max-abs does not drive the conclusions of this work. The task was to judge which story exhibits more gender stereotyping. The task followed forced pairwise comparison design. neither option was deliberately excluded to enforce discriminative judgment in every case, as relative comparisons reduce subjectivity relative to absolute rating scales. A.7.2 Annotation Sheet Format Each annotation row contained the fields: pair_id, occupation, gender, story_A, story_B, and label (A or B). Annotation was conducted in two language-specific files, English and Hindi, with task instructions provided in the respective language. The label schema was kept identical across both languages to enable cross-lingual comparison. A.7.3 Inter-Annotator Agreement Three independent annotators labeled each of the 50 story pairs per language (100 pairs total). The 50 pairs per language constitute stratified validation sample, balanced across gender (25 male, 25 female) and sampled uniformly across occupations, and are used here to validate annotation reliability. For downstream analysis, we used majority vote as the final label for each pair.
